stories.title: Financial Adviser SFA Agency Grows to 14K SF at Rudin’s One Battery Park Plaza
stories.excerpt: Financial advisory firm SFA Agency has expanded its footprint and extended its lifespan at Rudin’s One Battery Park Plaza in Manhattan’s Financial District, Commercial Observer has learned. SFA Agency, which is an affiliate of Northwestern Mutual, signed a 10-year lease extension and expanded by 5,170 square feet at the property to occupy a total of 14,071 square feet across the building’s 25th floor, according to Rudin. The deal represents a relocation within One Battery Park Plaza for the financial services company, which moved onto the building’s 33rd floor in 2023 and occupied an initial 8,901 square feet in a five-and-a-half year deal. SFA Agency will move to its larger space on the 25th floor in early 2027, Rudin said. The asking rent for the new deal was not disclosed, but the average asking rent for office space in Lower Manhattan was $77.76 per square foot in May, according to data from Colliers. Kevin Daly, vice president of office leasing at Rudin, represented the landlord in the deal. “SFA Agency’s long-term extension and expansion is a direct reflection of our commitment to superior customer service,” Daly said in a statement. “As evidenced by the leasing success this year and reaching virtual full occupancy at One Battery Park Plaza, there remains a strong and growing market appetite for well-located and high-quality office space in Lower Manhattan.” Scott Vinett from Savills represented SFA Agency in the lease expansion. “SFA Agency’s decision to expand and extend at One Battery Park Plaza reflects both the continued growth of its business and the strength of the building as a long-term workplace solution,” Vinett said in a statement. Built in 1971, One Battery Park Plaza is a 35-story, 800,000-square-foot office tower overlooking Battery Park. Other tenants in the building include government agency the New York City Independent Budget Office, law firm Skarzynski Marick & Black and talent acquisition software provider Radancy. Amanda Schiavo can b
bullets: SFA Agency (Northwestern Mutual affiliate) expands 5,170 SF at Rudin's One Battery Park Plaza, Manhattan Financial District
10-year lease extension; tenant relocates from 33rd to 25th floor in early 2027
Market signal: Lower Manhattan office at $77.76/SF asking in May; financial services firm commits long-term despite office headwinds
stories.summary: SFA Agency, a Northwestern Mutual affiliate, signed a 10-year lease extension and expanded by 5,170 square feet at One Battery Park Plaza in Manhattan's Financial District, bringing total occupancy to 14,071 SF on the 25th floor. The tenant previously occupied 8,901 SF on the 33rd floor under a 5.5-year deal signed in 2023. The move signals tenant confidence in Lower Manhattan despite broader office sector weakness, with average asking rents around $77.76/SF.
stories.impact: Long-term financial services commitment in Lower Manhattan suggests selective flight-to-quality among large institutional tenants; implies downtown Manhattan Class-A assets retain tenant stickiness and ability to command premium rents, supporting valuations for REIT trophy assets, but also signals broader office-to-multifamily conversion opportunities in secondary/tertiary Manhattan office buildings.

stories.title: Dallas Office Highrise Hits the Market
stories.excerpt: The Newmark Group has the listing for North Dallas’ Hillcrest Tower, a 168,000-square-foot office property at 12720 Hillcrest along Interstate 635. The property is 97% leased. The Dallas Business Journal reports the 10-story tower has more than 95,000 square feet of new and renewed leases since 2024. Hillcrest Tower was built in 1982. Tenants in the building include primarily medical, real estate and professional services companies. The Dallas County Appraisal District values the land and the building at $17.8 million. No asking price is listed. The property is owned by Albany Road Real Estate, which has held the property since 2017. Albany Road also owns the 448,000-square-foot Mockingbird Towers at 1341 W. Mockingbird Lane in Dallas, which it bought in 2020. Hillcrest Tower is located relatively close to Medical City Dallas, NorthPark Center, Preston Center, Galleria Dallas and is just north of the former Valley View Mall site that could be home to the Dallas Mavericks new arena and entertainment district. The post Dallas Office Highrise Hits the Market appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Hillcrest Tower, Dallas North (I-635 corridor), 168K SF office; 97% leased, 95K+ SF new/renewed since 2024
Land and building appraised $17.8M; owner Albany Road held since 2017; 10-story 1982 vintage
Proximity to Medical City Dallas, NorthPark, and Valley View Mavericks arena site signals upside from entertainment/mixed-use redevelopment
stories.summary: Hillcrest Tower, a 168,000-SF office highrise in North Dallas near I-635, has been listed for sale at strong occupancy (97%) with over 95,000 SF of new and renewed leases since 2024. The 1982-vintage property is appraised at $17.8M and held by Albany Road Real Estate since 2017. Tenants include medical, real estate, and professional services firms.
stories.impact: Dallas North submarket office lease velocity and occupancy strength indicate continued demand from healthcare and professional services, but listing signals owner capital redeployment; proximity to planned Valley View Mavericks arena (mixed-use, entertainment district) creates longer-term land-value uplift risk and potential future multifamily/hospitality conversion catalyst if office demand softens post-arena completion.

stories.title: Nashville’s Population, Business Growth Spur Commercial Real Estate Momentum
stories.excerpt: Something is brewing in Nashville. In April, Starbucks announced plans to open a Southeast regional headquarters in Downtown Nashville that will house 2,000 employees. The $100 million investment that will involve leasing the entire 250,000-square-foot Peabody Union building is just the latest example of major corporations from a variety of industries noticing the Music City and gathering plenty of wind at the back of Nashville’s commercial real estate industry. “It has become a pretty balanced economy that isn’t necessarily that reliant on one specific sector of the economy like, say, Dallas or Austin is,” said Andrew Donchez, partner and head of development at SomeraRoad, who leads the investment and development firm’s Nashville office. “Health care is obviously a huge driver, but you’ve also got tons of tech [and] financial services with a number of big companies all looking at Nashville or already here.” Technology companies have increased their presence in Nashville — long known for its thriving health care and music industries — and that tech trend will be amplified once Oracle moves its global headquarters to the city’s East Bank neighborhood. The 70-acre Oracle campus slated for completion around 2031 is part of a $4.5 billion investment by Oracle CEO Larry Ellison to relocate its headquarters from Austin, Texas, creating 8,500 Nashville-area jobs. Music City’s CRE industry received more good news in early June when J.P. Morgan Chase announced expansion plans in Nashville with a 51,000-square-foot lease at 1600 West End Avenue in the city’s Broadwest area. AllianceBernstein was one of the first financial services companies to make a big bet on Nashville in 2018, when the global asset management firm announced it was moving its headquarters there from Manhattan. The company relocated more than 1,000 jobs in a move that was projected to save $80 million per year in overhead and operational costs, according to a 2022 Bloomberg analysis. Amazon also planted a fl
bullets: Starbucks opening Southeast HQ in Nashville Downtown: 2,000 employees, $100M investment, 250K SF Peabody Union lease
Nashville adding healthcare, tech, financial services alongside traditional music/entertainment; economy diversifying beyond single sectors
Corporate HQ anchors signal sustained in-migration and wage-earner growth ahead of broader demand realization
stories.summary: Starbucks announced plans for a Southeast regional headquarters in Downtown Nashville, committing 2,000 employees and $100 million in investment to lease the entire 250,000-SF Peabody Union building. The move reflects Nashville's emergence as a balanced economy with growing strength in healthcare, technology, and financial services—sectors moving beyond the city's traditional music and entertainment focus.
stories.impact: Starbucks HQ relocation to Nashville signals sustained in-migration of corporate jobs and high-income talent; development window is open for multifamily acquisition/underwriting in Nashville submarkets, particularly downtown and surrounding corridors, ahead of secondary and tertiary occupancy waves from employer backfill and family in-migration; rent growth potential significant in next 18–36 months.

stories.title: Bistro Pardon My French Sets Up Shop at Jeff Sutton’s 373 Broome Street
stories.excerpt: Ooh la la. French-inspired bistro Pardon My French is coming to Manhattan’s SoHo neighborhood after signing a 1,000-square-foot lease at 373 Broome Street, a property owned in a joint venture between Jeff Sutton’s Wharton Properties and Gorjian Real Estate Group, according to broker Meridian Capital Group. The asking rent for the ground-floor retail space was $225 per square foot. The length of the lease, which was signed at the end of April, was not disclosed. Both the landlord and the tenant were represented by Max Freudenberger and Noam Aziz from Meridian. “This deal is a strong signal of where the SoHo/Nolita market stands right now,” Freudenberger said in a statement. “Rents north of $200 per square foot for small-format food and beverage concepts reflect genuine operator confidence in this corridor. Pardon My French is a fantastic fit for the neighborhood, and we’re excited to see them bring their concept to Broome Street.” Pardon My French will close its doors at 103 Avenue B on June 30 following 11 years in the space. The restaurant made the announcement earlier this month via Instagram, but posted a hopeful message about its future on its website. “We are deeply grateful for every meal shared, every conversation held, and every moment spent together,” the eatery wrote on its website. “This is the end of one chapter. But not the end of the story. A new project is already taking shape, and we look forward to welcoming you again soon.” Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Pardon My French bistro signs 1K SF ground-floor lease at SoHo's 373 Broome Street (Sutton/Gorjian JV)
Asking rent $225/SF for small-format F&B; represents high-barrier-to-entry price signal for neighborhood
Broker cites genuine operator confidence in SoHo/Nolita corridor; tenant relocating from Avenue B after 11 years
stories.summary: French bistro Pardon My French signed a 1,000-SF ground-floor lease at 373 Broome Street in SoHo at $225/SF. The property is jointly owned by Jeff Sutton's Wharton Properties and Gorjian Real Estate Group. The deal, signed in late April, is notable for the high per-square-foot rent and represents new operator confidence in the SoHo/Nolita corridor, though the lease term was not disclosed.
stories.impact: SoHo retail rents north of $225/SF for small-format F&B indicate strong tourism and local demand recovery post-pandemic; signals retail-anchored mixed-use development is viable in Manhattan's gentrified neighborhoods, supporting valuations for ground-floor retail in multifamily developments; however, high rents may cannibalize smaller independents, reducing diversity.

stories.title: Kennedy Wilson, Fairfax Financial Close on $1.65B Take-Private Deal
stories.excerpt: Kennedy-Wilson Holdings, Inc. said Tuesday that Fairfax Financial Holdings Limited had completed its all-cash acquisition of the company. First announced this past February, the privatization deal is valued at $1.65 billion. William McMorrow, chairman and CEO of Kennedy Wilson, and senior management of Kennedy Wilson, collectively known as KW Management Group, maintain effective and operational control of the Beverly Hills-based real estate investment firm and its subsidiaries. Toronto-based Fairfax now has a majority of the economic interest in the company. In addition, Fairfax said Tuesday that a consortium led by KW Management Group and certain Fairfax affiliates has obtained a $1.3-billion term loan facility. Fairfax is providing a standby guarantee on the loan. With the completion of the acquisition, which was approved by Kennedy Wilson shareholders in a special meeting on June 10, Kennedy Wilson shares have ceased trading on the New York Stock Exchange. The post Kennedy Wilson, Fairfax Financial Close on $1.65B Take-Private Deal appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Fairfax Financial (Toronto) closes $1.65B all-cash take-private of Kennedy Wilson; completed June 2024
KW Management Group retains operational control; Fairfax holds majority economic interest; $1.3B term loan facility obtained
Privatization removes KW from public markets; shifts capital allocation flexibility and potential financing costs for future acquisitions
stories.summary: Fairfax Financial Holdings completed its $1.65 billion all-cash acquisition of Kennedy Wilson, a Beverly Hills-based real estate investment firm. The deal was approved by KW shareholders on June 10. KW Management Group, led by Chairman/CEO William McMorrow, retains operational control while Fairfax holds majority economic interest. A $1.3 billion term loan facility supports the transaction, with Fairfax providing a standby guarantee.
stories.impact: KW take-private removes a major publicly traded multifamily REIT from capital markets; Fairfax's long-term hold posture and Canadian ownership may reduce aggressive portfolio recycling and increase hold periods, potentially tightening secondary multifamily asset availability; however, non-public structure allows higher leverage and less quarterly earnings pressure, potentially increasing acquisition appetite in down-market periods.

stories.title: Fifield Pursuing Coral Gables Site for 9-Story Apartment Tower
stories.excerpt: Fifield Cos. is asking the Coral Gables planning board for permission to convert a school property into an apartment project called Chrystal Residences. The company is looking at a 1.47-acre site at 110 Phoenetia Ave, which is home to a 9,280-square-foot special needs facility. The South Florida Business Journal reports Fifield paid $16 million for the property last year. It wants to build a nine-story/93-foot building with 184 residential units for rent and 16 live-work units. There would be a pool deck and amenities on the ninth floor. The building would total 226,193 square feet. The project also includes space for the school. The two-story live-work units would be on the ground floor. The residential units would range from 656 to 1,299 square feet. There would be 88 one-bedroom units, 72 two-bedroom units, and 24 three-bedroom units. Corwil Architects designed the project. Join the region’s leading multifamily owners, developers, investors, lenders, and brokers at Connect Texas Multifamily on August 13 in Dallas for a focused discussion on the market forces redefining Texas real estate. Be in the room with the decision-makers driving deals and growth across Texas. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Fifield Pursuing Coral Gables Site for 9-Story Apartment Tower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Fifield Cos. seeking entitlement for 184-unit apartment tower at 110 Phoenetia Ave, Coral Gables (1.47 acres)
9-story/93-foot building; $16M land acquisition (2023); includes 16 live-work units and school facility retention
Unit mix: 88 one-bed, 72 two-bed, 24 three-bed; 226K SF total; 226K SF includes 88 one-bed, 72 two-bed, 24 three-bed; seeking entitlement approval from planning board
stories.summary: Fifield Cos. is pursuing entitlement for Chrystal Residences, a 9-story, 93-foot apartment tower on a 1.47-acre former special-needs school site at 110 Phoenetia Ave in Coral Gables. The developer paid $16 million for the property in 2023 and is proposing 184 rental units (88 one-beds, 72 two-beds, 24 three-beds) plus 16 live-work units across 226,193 SF, with pool and amenities on the ninth floor and retained school space.
stories.impact: Coral Gables institutional property redevelopment signals strong apartment demand in South Florida and zoning flexibility for mixed-use; successful entitlement would validate school-to-residential conversion model in Miami-Dade submarket; density bonus and live-work component suggest apartment + alternative tenure demand in high-barrier-to-entry South Florida markets.

stories.title: Coke Bottler Completes $84M Orlando Warehouse Upgrade
stories.excerpt: Coke Florida has completed an $84 million renovation of an Orlando distribution and bottling center. The 180,000-square-foot facility at 3095 Eunice Ave. was completely gutted and rebuilt to support new state-of-the-art equipment. The Orlando Business Journal reports the upgraded facility includes advanced automated warehouse technology designed to improve efficiency, order accuracy and overall service reliability. Coke Florida operates a nearby 392,000-square-foot facility at 2900 Mercy Drive that was built in 1969. Coke Florida is the sixth-largest Coca-Cola bottler in the U.S. with more than 5,000 employees, four manufacturing facilities and 18 sales and distribution centers across the state. Central Florida is home to nearly 1,000 Coke Florida associates across sales, manufacturing, distribution and professional services. The company ranks as the eighth-largest private beverage fleet in the U.S.,. The post Coke Bottler Completes $84M Orlando Warehouse Upgrade appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Coke Florida completed $84M renovation of 180K SF Orlando distribution/bottling center at 3095 Eunice Ave
New automated warehouse tech; facility completely gutted and rebuilt; company also operates 392K SF facility built in 1969
Coke Florida: 5K+ employees, four manufacturing facilities, 18 distribution centers across state; top beverage fleet operator
stories.summary: Coke Florida completed an $84 million renovation of a 180,000-SF distribution and bottling facility in Orlando, completely gutting and rebuilding it with advanced automated warehouse technology to improve efficiency and order accuracy. Coke Florida operates a second 392,000-SF facility in Orlando built in 1969 and is the sixth-largest Coca-Cola bottler in the U.S. with 5,000+ employees across four manufacturing and 18 distribution centers statewide.
stories.impact: Large-scale industrial capex in Orlando signals strong logistics demand and beverage industry confidence in Florida; modernization of distribution infrastructure typically precedes or follows significant employment growth; increased automation may reduce direct labor but signals larger supply-chain investment in region, supporting industrial multifamily demand nearby and general economic stability in Central Florida.

stories.title: JP Morgan, Freehill Capital Partners Form Industrial Acquisition Joint Venture
stories.excerpt: J.P. Morgan continues to push its chips into industrial real estate. Commercial Observer can first report that J.P. Morgan Asset Management and Freehill Capital Partners, an industrial real estate investment firm, announced a new joint venture Tuesday to acquire manufacturing-oriented industrial facilities across Houston and with access to the Port of Houston. J.P. Morgan will hold a 95 percent interest in the joint venture, while Freehill will round out the other 5 percent, according to a release. The amount of investment capital behind the partnership was not disclosed. Chad Tredway, global head of real estate at J.P. Morgan Asset Management, said in a statement that with increased onshoring and reshoring activity across the U.S., as well as the enormous investment demand tied to artificial intelligence and data center infrastructure, manufacturing-oriented industrial real estate is poised to perform well in the long run via “sustained growth.” “This initiative reinforces our strong conviction in the manufacturing sector and provides us an opportunity to create value for our clients and communities,” said Tredway. The joint venture will prioritize industrial sites with high power capacity, strong truck access, crane-ready functionality and outdoor storage, and will seek to invest in the maintenance and modernization of these buildings, according to a release. “With limited new supply and tenant needs increasingly centered on specialized features, we look forward to identifying opportunities for our investors in a segment that can be difficult to access at scale,” said Preston Meyer, portfolio manager at J.P. Morgan Asset Management. The joint venture is already getting busy. The JP Morgan and Freehill partnership announced that it acquired a 30,000-square-foot industrial building that sits on 2.3 acres at 18401 Intercontinental Crossing Drive in North Houston. Terms of that deal were not disclosed. Adam Hawkins, president of Freehill Capital Partners, said in a st
bullets: JP Morgan Asset Management and Freehill Capital Partners form JV for manufacturing-oriented industrial acquisitions; Houston/Port of Houston focus
JP Morgan holds 95% interest; Freehill 5%; investment amount not disclosed; targets onshoring/reshoring beneficiaries
Rationale: onshoring, reshoring, AI, and data-center infrastructure drive long-term manufacturing industrial growth thesis
stories.summary: JP Morgan Asset Management and Freehill Capital Partners announced a new joint venture to acquire manufacturing-oriented industrial facilities in Houston with Port of Houston access. JP Morgan holds a 95% interest and Freehill holds 5%. The partnership positions to capitalize on onshoring, reshoring, and AI/data-center infrastructure investment trends, though the total investment capital was not disclosed.
stories.impact: JP Morgan's manufacturing-industrial focus in Houston signals institutional belief in onshoring/reshoring durability and Port of Houston logistics value; increased capital chasing industrial in Houston may raise land and asset prices, squeezing development margins; however, port-adjacent industrial demand creates stable industrial real estate values that de-risk nearby multifamily anchors and reduce supply-chain-disruption uncertainty for multifamily financing.

stories.title: Investor Offloads Dunwoody Office Park for $27M
stories.excerpt: Grubb Properties sold the 284,000 square foot Park at Perimeter Center East to Network Realty Partners for $27.15 million. The Atlanta Business Chronicle reports Grubb is moving its Atlanta office from that Dunwoody property to an office it owns at 2400 Lake Park Drive in Smyrna. Grubb Properties paid $32.65 million for the 19.4-acre site in 2015 and has since tried to redevelop the property, but its plans have never been approved by local officials. Grubb Properties unveiled plans in 2018 for 900 for-sale condos and 500,000 square feet of office space just east of Perimeter Mall and north of Interstate 285, but it never moved forward. Tenants at Park at Permiter Center East include the Alzheimer’s Association, Assurance Financial, Bedrock Homes, the Consulate General of Argentina and Hanyard Law Office, P.C. The post Investor Offloads Dunwoody Office Park for $27M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Grubb Properties sold 284K SF Park at Perimeter Center East (Dunwoody, Atlanta) for $27.15M to Network Realty Partners
Grubb paid $32.65M in 2015; sought redevelopment (2018 plan: 900 condos, 500K SF office) but never gained local approval
Sale at 17% loss over 9 years signals office redevelopment failure and capital redeployment to company's own office (Smyrna)
stories.summary: Grubb Properties sold the 284,000-SF Park at Perimeter Center East in Dunwoody to Network Realty Partners for $27.15 million, a loss from its $32.65 million purchase in 2015. Grubb had attempted redevelopment in 2018 with a plan for 900 for-sale condos and 500K SF of office space but never secured local approval. Grubb is relocating its Atlanta office to a property it owns in Smyrna.
stories.impact: Dunwoody office-park sale at loss after failed redevelopment signals weak office-to-residential conversion economics in suburban Atlanta periphery and local zoning resistance; sale-at-loss indicates office asset values eroding faster than cap-rate expansion can offset; Grubb's capital redeployment out of peripheral office to owned asset suggests opportunity for multifamily developers to acquire underperforming office parks at distressed prices in Atlanta suburbs, though zoning friction remains a risk.

stories.title: Thiago da Costa of Procore: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: In May, construction technology giant Procore announced it had introduced a new suite of agentic artificial intelligence to expand its Procore AI platform. The added AI is powered by Datagrid Intelligence, a company Procore acquired in January, and which it has now integrated into its tech platform. The expanded platform introduces AI capabilities for high-friction construction workflows, including submittals, requests for information, contract reviews, and searches across drawings and specifications. The added AI is designed to execute work directly inside Procore using new “actions” and “triggers” capabilities that can coordinate workflows and respond automatically to project events. Thiago da Costa, senior vice president of AI and data at Procore, and former founder and CEO of Datagrid, spoke with Commercial Observer in May about Datagrid’s integration and what it means for Procore’s construction clients. This interview has been edited for length and clarity. Commercial Observer: Procore has added an agentic AI to its platform with the acquisition of Datagrid. What does that mean for clients, practically? Tiago da Costa: Taking a step back, Procore is the dominant record and system of action in construction. The majority of general contractors leverage Procore to build and to execute projects. What we are seeing with the advent of AI is how do we get rid of repetitive work people need to be doing that is also complex and requires them to think through that work. Engineers, project managers, all those folks are working inside Procore. They’re trying to cross-reference the documents and execute the project, making decisions where every decision has a huge impact on financial, labor, cost, schedule and so on. So, what we’re trying to do for our builders is to bring the best AI solution into that. Since the acquisition, we worked in record time to bring state-of-the-art AI technology and workflows inside the Procore environment, because the majority of th
bullets: Procore (construction tech) integrated Datagrid Intelligence acquisition into AI platform; released agentic AI suite in May
New AI handles submittals, RFI, contract reviews, drawing searches; includes 'actions' and 'triggers' for workflow automation
Procore dominates construction software; AI expansion targets high-friction workflows to reduce project friction and speed decision cycles
stories.summary: Construction technology company Procore introduced an expanded agentic AI platform in May, powered by Datagrid Intelligence (acquired January 2024). The AI adds capabilities for automating submittals, requests for information, contract reviews, and drawing/specification searches, with new "actions" and "triggers" that coordinate workflows and respond to project events automatically. Procore is the dominant construction software platform serving the industry.
stories.impact: Procore's AI-driven workflow automation will compress construction timelines and reduce RFI/submittal delays, lowering soft costs for apartment developers; faster project delivery could accelerate multifamily supply growth if labor remains available; however, workflow automation may also expose labor bottlenecks (trades, inspectors) that AI cannot solve, potentially capping time savings and limiting supply acceleration.

stories.title: Stars CEO says tear down American Airlines Center once teams leave
stories.excerpt: Dallas Stars CEO Brad Alberts is pitching a plan for what should happen when the Stars and Mavericks leave the 25-year-old American Airlines Center: rip it down to the studs.  In an interview with the Dallas Business Journal, Alberts endorsed the idea that once the Mavs and Stars vacate their long-time downtown Dallas home, it should be demolished. Three arenas within 15 miles of each other is too much, Alberts said, which would be the scenario after the NBA and NHL franchise teams build their own stadiums. The Mavericks have publicly committed to Valley View for their new arena, which […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Dallas Stars CEO Brad Alberts endorses demolishing American Airlines Center once teams leave (leases expire 2031)
Mavericks committed to Valley View arena; Stars to Plano arena/entertainment complex ($3B, Centennial/Cawley/Plano deal); three arenas within 15 miles deemed excessive
Plano offering Stars 100% revenue control; City Council approved; traffic studies and prep underway for 2031+ construction
stories.summary: Dallas Stars CEO Brad Alberts stated that the American Airlines Center should be demolished once the Stars and Mavericks leave for new facilities. Both teams' leases expire in 2031. The Mavericks are committed to Valley View (within Dallas city limits), and the Stars are committed to a new $3 billion arena-and-entertainment complex in Plano, with Plano City Council approval and Centennial Real Estate/Cawley Partners as developers. Alberts noted three arenas within 15 miles would be excessive.
stories.impact: AAC demolition plan signals major downtown Dallas real estate disruption post-2031; loss of arena as anchor tenant and employment hub will pressure downtown residential and retail demand; however, Valley View Mavericks arena and Plano Stars complex create massive mixed-use development pipelines that will attract new demographic and spending anchors; multifamily developers should evaluate downtown Dallas conversion/ground-up opportunities in anticipation of AAC demolition and simultaneous entertainment-district construction in Valley View and Plano.

stories.title: Massachusetts relaunches effort to redevelop Boston Government Center
stories.excerpt: The Boston Government Center is getting dolled up for a redevelopment — again. Gov. Maura Healey’s administration released an offering memorandum for the Erich Lindemann Mental Health Center and the Charles F. Hurley Building in Boston’s West End neighborhood, Bisnow reported. The parcels form a 6.5-acre office complex in need of a revamp. “We’re turning underutilized state property into new homes, preserving important parts of this historic site, and ensuring that critical mental health services continue uninterrupted,” Healey said in a statement. The 1960s-built buildings have suffered in recent years as they’ve aged. The Lindemann building alone is expected to […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Massachusetts relaunches Boston Government Center redevelopment (6.5-acre West End site); offering memo released for Lindemann and Hurley buildings
Previous 2022 Leggat McCall deal ($1B mixed-use, 200 units, lab space) collapsed when life sciences market crashed
Hurley Building 347K SF (empty); Lindemann 1960s-vintage needs $300M+ capex; 115 mental health beds must relocate or stay on-site; affordability requirements mandated
stories.summary: Massachusetts Governor Maura Healey's administration released an offering memorandum for the Erich Lindemann Mental Health Center and Charles F. Hurley Building in Boston's West End, a 6.5-acre complex in need of redevelopment. A prior 2022 effort by Leggat McCall Properties for a $1 billion mixed-use project (200 units, lab space) collapsed when the life sciences market declined. The Hurley Building (347K SF) is now empty; Lindemann requires $300M+ in capital improvements and houses 115 mental health beds that must be retained or relocated.
stories.impact: Boston Government Center redevelopment restart signals continued appetite for public-land mixed-use projects despite prior life-sciences-driven failure; life-sciences use collapse suggests future proposals will emphasize housing (potentially rent-restricted due to city affordability mandates) and office rather than biotech; developers bidding should expect strong affordability requirements; complex mental health bed relocation/retention requirements may complicate timeline and increase soft costs; window exists for apartment-heavy proposals if lab/office component can be de-emphasized.

stories.title: Miami Wilds water park project crashes after judge nixes dev site lease due to “mutual mistake”
stories.excerpt: A controversial mixed-use water park project near Zoo Miami that’s been in the works for over a decade has collapsed, after a judge voided the development site lease.  The developers –– Paul M. Lambert, Bernard Zyscovich and Michael Diaz Jr. –– working through the entity Miami Wilds LLC planned a water park, a 200-key hotel and between 15,000 and 20,000 square feet of commercial and dining space on a 27.5-acre site they had leased from the county, according to Lambert. Miami Wilds was to revamp and manage another 38.5 acres of parking lots to be shared between the development and […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Wilds water park project (10+ years in development) collapsed; court voided site lease due to 'mutual mistake'
Developers: Paul Lambert, Bernard Zyscovich, Michael Diaz Jr.; proposed 27.5-acre site: water park, 200-key hotel, 15K-20K SF retail/dining; 38.5 acres parking shared with Zoo Miami
Lawsuit/litigation tied to endangered species claims; land is part of Richmond Pine Rockland habitat, one of largest outside Everglades; Miami-Dade county sided with conservationists
stories.summary: The Miami Wilds mixed-use water park project near Zoo Miami, in development for over a decade, collapsed after a judge voided the development site lease due to "mutual mistake." Developers Paul Lambert, Bernard Zyscovich, and Michael Diaz Jr. had proposed a 27.5-acre site with a water park, 200-room hotel, and 15,000-20,000 SF of retail/dining, along with 38.5 acres of shared parking. The project was embroiled in lawsuits over endangered species and Richmond Pine Rockland habitat protection, with Miami-Dade County ultimately supporting conservationists.
stories.impact: Miami Wilds collapse reflects high litigation and environmental permitting risk for large mixed-use developments on marginal/sensitive land in South Florida; conservationist opposition and county support for environmental protection signal tightening development constraints in Miami-Dade County outside core urban areas; developers should focus on in-fill urban and already-permitted sites and avoid greenfield projects in biodiverse or wetland-adjacent locations; loss of water-park/hotel mixed-use anchor removes potential demand driver for nearby multifamily.

stories.title: Belmont developer plans 202 apartments in neighboring San Mateo
stories.excerpt: The McLellan Company is moving forward with multifamily plans in San Mateo.  The Belmont-based developer has filed formal application permits for a six-story residential building at 4095 Pacific Boulevard in San Mateo, close to the city’s border with Belmont, San Francisco YIMBY reported. McLellan is listed as both the project applicant and property owner in legal documents, per S.F. YIMBY.  When complete, the development will deliver 202 units in a 79-foot tall structure at the corner of Pacific Boulevard and Laurie Meadows Drive, across the street from Caltrain tracks roughly halfway between the Belmont and Hillsdale stations. The edifice will […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: McLellan Company filing permits for 202-unit apartment building at 4095 Pacific Boulevard, San Mateo (6-story, 79 feet)
San Mateo state-mandated housing goal: 7K+ units by 2031; development includes 24 studios, 111 one-beds, 67 two-beds; 25 units deed-restricted BMR
SB 330 and density bonus streamline review; existing gas station and shopping center demolished; parking: 207 cars, 234 bikes
stories.summary: The McLellan Company filed formal permits for a 202-unit apartment building at 4095 Pacific Boulevard in San Mateo, a six-story, 79-foot structure on 1.6 acres. The project includes 24 studios, 111 one-bedrooms, and 67 two-bedrooms, with 25 units deed-restricted as below-market-rate housing. SB 330 and state density bonus law streamline environmental and zoning review. An existing gas station and shopping center will be demolished.
stories.impact: San Mateo housing mandate (7K+ units by 2031) is driving streamlined multifamily approval pipeline; SB 330 and density bonus law are effectively reducing entitlement risk and timeline for apartment developers statewide; McLellan's 202-unit project advances San Mateo toward goals; successful delivery will validate Peninsula development model and likely trigger follow-on projects in San Mateo and nearby Belmont; multifamily developers should expect heightened competition and land cost inflation in Bay Area gateway submarkets as mandate deadlines approach.

stories.title: Bachow seeks boost in density for downtown Fort Lauderdale tower
stories.excerpt: Bachow Ventures is looking to squeeze more value out of its Fort Lauderdale tower, proposing 62 additional units as part of a revised plan to increase the development’s density.  The Miami-based developer first secured approval in April 2023 for a proposed redevelopment of the 33,600-square-foot lot at 11 North Andrews Avenue. That proposal was for a 244-foot tall mixed-use development with 23 stories and 316 residential units.  In December 2023, the city approved an amendment to the proposal, allowing for an additional 14 stories reaching 385 feet tall and 1,500 square feet of commercial space.  Now Bachow has revised its […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Bachow Ventures revising Fort Lauderdale tower at 11 North Andrews Ave; adding 62 units, shrinking height from 385 to 293 feet
Revised plan: 27 stories, 378 total units (vs. 316 initial); seeking zoning relief: floorplate 12.5K to 20K SF, tower setback 35 to 27 feet, tower spacing 30 to 25 feet
City vote June 16; Bachow acquired site for $8M in 2023; land price $147.8K in 1997 indicates significant value appreciation in 25 years
stories.summary: Bachow Ventures revised its Fort Lauderdale residential development at 11 North Andrews Avenue, proposing 62 additional units while reducing tower height from 385 to 293 feet across 27 stories. The revised plan seeks zoning amendments to increase maximum floorplate from 12,500 to 20,000 SF, decrease tower spacing from 30 to 25 feet, and reduce setback from 35 to 27 feet. The project would total 378 units and 4,800 SF of commercial space if approved in the June 16 city vote. Bachow acquired the site for $8 million in 2023.
stories.impact: Bachow's density-upside revision signals strong Fort Lauderdale apartment demand and feasibility of higher entitlement approval standards in Regional Activity Center zoning; zoning relief strategy (floorplate and setback reductions) is increasingly accepted in Florida coastal markets; successful approval would validate high-density downtown Fort Lauderdale development model; ongoing density creep in Fort Lauderdale expands addressable multifamily market and justifies continued acquisition activity in downtown corridors.

stories.title: Zeckendorf, Atlas Capital’s 80 Clarkson inks $80M contract
stories.excerpt: Zeckendorf Development and Atlas Capital Group’s downtown condo project has scored another big deal. The project snagged a contract for a duplex penthouse asking $80 million in its west tower, the Wall Street Journal reported. If the deal closes at the price, it would set a record price per square foot mark for a home sold Downtown at $11,236.  It is also the second priciest contract signed in New York City this year, after a condo asking $89 million at Legion Investment Group and Nahla Capital’s project at 1122 Madison Avenue found a buyer.  At 400 feet in the air, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Zeckendorf Development and Atlas Capital's 80 Clarkson (downtown NYC condo) signs $80M duplex penthouse contract; second-highest NYC deal YTD
Unit: 7.1K SF, 4-bed/6-bath, 900+ SF private outdoor space, highest condo in West Village history at 400 feet elevation
Price-per-SF record for Downtown: $11.2K/SF; 112-unit project has closed $1B+ in sales; previous high contract: $129M (December)
stories.summary: Zeckendorf Development and Atlas Capital Group's downtown Manhattan condo project 80 Clarkson signed an $80 million contract for a duplex penthouse in the west tower. The 7,100-SF unit includes four bedrooms, six bathrooms, and over 900 SF of private outdoor space, setting a Downtown record price-per-SF of $11,236 and ranking as NYC's second-priciest contract signed in 2024. The 112-unit project has generated over $1 billion in sales.
stories.impact: 80 Clarkson's $11.2K/SF Downtown record signals sustained ultra-luxury apartment demand in Manhattan's gentrified neighborhoods and high feasibility of prestige rental conversions; transaction strength implies strong equity capital availability for multifamily development and supports continued investment in trophy assets; however, concentrate wealth in luxury segment may mask weakness in middle-market apartment fundamentals and should not inform supply-demand assumptions for workforce/affordable-housing development.
